alfabetizzazione-delle-reti-come-compren.english: Alphabetization of networks: How to understand, design and read visual relational models. Models help us understand complexity by offering simplified structures that make otherwise invisible social dynamics readable. In this context, networks are distinguished by their ability to represent relational information: nodes and connections reduce society to actors and connections, bringing out patterns that often remain hidden in our eyes. From the 18th century the networks have passed from being curious mathematical objects to essential tools in many disciplines. The first network visualizations revealed patterns of friendship in class highlighting social ties, while today computational studies map a great diversity of data, from cooking recipes to scientific collaborations. With their visual grammar, the networks today invite us to compare, classification and interpretation in a multitude of disciplines. However, their diffusion also brings risks: the networks can be exchanged for objective spaces, the central nodes for the most important, and the density admired for the aesthetics. To face these visual challenges we need a new form of literacy. Literacy of networks can be defined as the ability of literacy, and the ability of literacy of the networks can be defined by defining them. It traces the history of networks from mathematical origins to their integration in digital media, showing a new way of relational reading. The article explores three dimensions of networks: the design choices that shape the meaning, the spatial thought that guides interpretation and experimental projects that transform visualization into performance practice. By placing networks at the intersection of information design, critical research and cultural practice, the article argues that spreading the literacy of networks is essential to confront the design of knowledge in today's social fabric.

immaginare-gesti-barriera-contro-il-rito.english: Imagine gestures-barrier against the return to pre-crisis production. There may be something inappropriate in looking at the post-crisis era when health care workers are still at the forefront of the crisis, millions of people are losing their jobs, and many grieving families cannot even bury their deaths. Yet, it is precisely this time to fight for economic recovery, once the crisis is over, not to bring us back to the same old climate regime against which we have tried, without success, to fight. Indeed, the health crisis is included in what is not a crisis, always passing by definition, but rather a lasting and irreversible ecological mutation. If we have good chances of exiting from the first, we have very few ways to escape from the second. The two situations are not on the same scale of magnitude, but it remains illuminating to articulate the one with the other. In any case, it would be a sin not to reflect on the health crisis to discover other ways of entering the ecological mutation, rather than do it blindly.
